lesson.menu_desc: Reading a real QBR executive summary + consolidating every structure -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 -- Capstone: Quarterly Business Review (QBR)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patrícia Xavier Lima Iwashita | Aula 20 -- Capstone: Quarterly Business Review (QBR)

paras: This quarterly business review covers the third quarter for Northwind Logistics, one of our premium accounts. The goal of a QBR is simple: to look back at the results, to align on what matters most, and to plan the next quarter together. Over the last three months, the team has made strong progress. Adoption has grown across all departments, and the account is now on track to reach the objectives we set at the kickoff.

The highlights are clear. Compared to last quarter, the adoption rate has increased from sixty to eighty percent, and the health score is higher than ever. The support team has resolved every critical ticket within the agreed deadline. In our last call, the customer said that the new walkthrough had saved their users a lot of time. There is one honest concern: two teams in finance are still slow to adopt, so their usage is lower than the rest.

Based on these results, we have two recommendations for next quarter. First, we suggest a short extra training for the finance teams; if they get more support, their adoption will catch up quickly. Second, because the partnership has delivered real value, we recommend that the customer renew the contract early and add ten new licenses. If the customer agreed to a longer contract, we could offer a better price. Finally, we will share a written success plan with clear objectives, and we will review the progress again in the next QBR.
reading.source: Adapted for class use from a customer success Quarterly Business Review

gist.prompt: What is the best one-line summary of this QBR?
choices: It is a price list of new software licenses for Northwind Logistics.
It reviews the quarter's results and highlights, raises one honest concern, and recommends clear next steps for the account.
It is a support ticket about a critical bug in the finance team.

items: This quarter, adoption has grown across all departments.
The summary says adoption has grown across all departments and the account is on track.
Every team is adopting the product at the same speed.
Two teams in finance are still slow to adopt, so their usage is lower than the rest.
The team recommends that the customer renew the contract early and add new licenses.
The recommendations are an early renewal plus ten new licenses, because the partnership delivered value.
The QBR suggests no plan at all for the next quarter.
It promises a written success plan with clear objectives and a review in the next QBR.
This QBR mixes good news with one honest concern. Why is it important to share the concern instead of hiding it?
The team recommends an early renewal because the partnership delivered value. In your own accounts, how do you show value before a renewal?
If you ran this QBR, which one extra metric would you add to prove that adoption is really growing?

listenings.text: Hi Patricia, this is David from Northwind, just a quick note before our quarterly business review tomorrow. First, thank you, because this has been a strong quarter for my teams. I have seen the adoption numbers, and honestly they are better than I expected. On the call, I would like you to focus on three things. First, walk us through the highlights, especially how the support team handled those critical tickets, because my director will ask about that. Then, I want your honest view on the finance teams; their usage is still low, and I need a clear plan to fix it before I take this to the board. After that, we should talk about next year. If the value is there, I am open to renewing the contract early and adding more licenses, but I will need a strong business case to justify the cost to my boss. So please come with your recommendations and your numbers ready. See you tomorrow, and thanks again for the partnership.

Hi Patricia, it is Karen, your manager. I had a look at your slides for the Northwind QBR, and they are in great shape, so well done. I just want to share a few tips before you present. First, lead with the results, not the agenda; open with your strongest highlight, the jump in adoption, so the room is on your side from minute one. Then, when you reach the finance concern, do not hide it, but always pair the problem with a recommendation; say what you have noticed and what you suggest in the same breath. For the renewal, remember to talk about value before you talk about price; once the customer sees the return on investment, the contract conversation becomes much easier. And if David pushes back on the cost, stay calm and bring it back to the business case. You know this account better than anyone, so trust yourself. Go and show them what a real partnership looks like. Good luck, I know you will be great.
